title: Build Day 2
subtitle: Polish, test, and prepare your story for the world
overview: Day 9 is about taking your rough prototype from yesterday and making it something you are proud to show. Focus on three things: fixing your biggest known problem, improving the user experience, and preparing a compelling 3-minute presentation for tomorrow's Demo Day.

Real user testing is the secret weapon today. Watching someone new try your project tells you far more than guessing what they'll think — and a clear, well-rehearsed pitch is what turns a good project into a memorable one.
objectives: Fix the top issue identified in yesterday's build log
Improve the user experience — is it easy for someone new to use?
Conduct a second round of user testing and gather feedback

concepts.heading: How to Run a Proper User Test
body: User testing is not asking "do you like it?" — that gets you polite answers, not useful ones. To learn the truth, you give a real tester a real task and watch what actually happens, without rescuing them.
Follow this protocol with each tester: set up first, observe without helping, then ask the same five questions at the end.
bullets: BEFORE — Set up: tell the tester "We are testing the tool, not you. There are no wrong answers. Please think out loud as you use it."
BEFORE — Give them a specific task: "Imagine you need to [specific use case]. Try using this tool to accomplish that."
DURING — Observe, don't help: do NOT rescue them when they get stuck. Their confusion is your most valuable data.
DURING — Note exactly where they pause, hesitate, or make mistakes, and write down their exact words when they react.
AFTER — Ask: "What would you use this for if you had it right now?"
AFTER — Ask: "What confused you most?" and "What was the most useful part?"
AFTER — Ask: "What one thing would you change?" and "Would you recommend this to a friend? Why or why not?"

text: "Do you like it?" gets you politeness. A real task gets you the truth.
When a tester gets stuck, don't help — that confusion is the most useful thing you'll learn all day.

activities.name: Demo Day Presentation Template
intro: Build your 3-minute presentation around these four sections. Practise out loud and time yourself — 3 minutes goes fast.
steps: Section 1 — The Problem (45 sec): open with a real scenario, "Imagine you are [user] and you face [problem]..." Make the audience feel the problem before you show the solution.

Section 2 — Our Solution (60 sec): show, don't just tell — do a live demo or screenshot walkthrough. Explain what the AI does versus what the human does, and highlight the most impressive or surprising thing it can do.
Section 3 — What We Learned (45 sec): what was the hardest thing to build? What did the AI do that surprised you? What would you improve with more time?
Section 4 — The Bigger Picture (30 sec): if this tool existed fully developed, what impact could it have? Close with your team's key insight from the project.
facilitator: Hold a strict timer during the dry-run — going over 3 minutes is the most common problem and the easiest to fix with practice.
Push every team to include a live demo moment rather than only talking about the project.

activities.name: Project One-Pager
intro: Complete this one-pager — it will be displayed at Demo Day so visitors can understand your project at a glance.
steps: What We Would Build Next (if we had more time):
QR code or link to try our project: [add here]
facilitator: Provide a printable template or slide so all one-pagers look consistent at the exhibition.
Encourage teams to add a QR code linking to a live version of their project where possible.

reflection: What was the single biggest issue from your build log, and did you fix it today?
Watching a new user, where did they get confused — and what does that tell you to change?
Which of the four presentation sections feels strongest, and which needs more practice?
What is the one key insight you want the audience to remember tomorrow?

vocab.definition: Watching real people use your project to find out what works and what confuses them.
How easy and pleasant something is for a new person to use.
Asking a tester to say their thoughts out loud as they use a tool, so you hear their reasoning.
A single-page summary of a project's problem, solution, and key facts.
A short, persuasive presentation of your project and why it matters.

quiz.q: Why is "Do you like it?" a bad user-testing question?
options: It gets you polite answers instead of useful information about what actually confuses people
It takes too long to answer
Testers are not allowed to answer yes-or-no questions
It only works for chatbots, not other projects
quiz.explain: People want to be nice, so "do you like it?" gets polite answers. Giving a real task and watching what happens reveals the truth.

quiz.q: During a user test, you should jump in and help the tester as soon as they get stuck.
quiz.explain: False. You observe without helping — where the tester gets stuck or confused is your most valuable data for improving the project.

quiz.q: What is the correct order of sections in the 3-minute Demo Day presentation?
options: The Problem → Our Solution → What We Learned → The Bigger Picture
Our Solution → The Problem → The Bigger Picture → What We Learned
What We Learned → The Problem → Our Solution → The Bigger Picture
The Bigger Picture → Our Solution → The Problem → What We Learned
quiz.explain: You start with the Problem so the audience cares, then show your Solution, share what you Learned, and finish with the Bigger Picture.

quiz.q: In your solution section, it's a good idea to show a live demo rather than only describing the project.
quiz.explain: True. Showing beats telling — a live demo or screenshot walkthrough is far more convincing than just talking about what your project does.

rows: Stand-up: share Day 8 build log — biggest issue to fix today
Fix Sprint — address your top issue from yesterday
User Testing Round 2 — test with 2 people outside your team
Polish + Demo Prep — rehearse your 3-minute pitch
Teams present dry-run to facilitator for feedback
